data.title: Agents in Pretti shooting on administrative leave
data.description:
The federal agents who fatally shot Alex Pretti were placed on administrative leave Saturday, a Department of Homeland Security (DHS) official told NewsNation, the Hill’s sister network. 

A Border Patrol agent and a Customs and Border Protection (CBP) officer shot and killed Pretti in Minneapolis on Saturday. He was the second person killed by federal law enforcement during the Trump administration’s immigration crackdown in Minnesota. An Immigration and Customs Enforcement officer shot Renee Good, a 37-year-old mother, in her car on Jan. 7. 

The DHS source told NewsNation on Wednesday that the decision to place the officers on three days of administrative leave is standard policy for officer-involved shootings. This differs from restrictive duty in that the agents will not lose their gun, badge or pay, the official noted.  

A CBP preliminary report on the shooting was shared with the House Homeland Security and Judiciary committees on Tuesday, The Associated Press reported. 

According to this report, the shooting occurred when the CBP officials attempted to detain Pretti and discovered he was carrying a firearm. The Border Patrol agent removed the gun and then both the agent and CBP officer fired several shots at Pretti. The report stated federal officers provided medical care to him before he was transported to a hospital. 

DHS has defended the officers who shot him as acting in self-defense, and the Trump administration has criticized Pretti for carrying a firearm — arguments that have drawn criticism from both sides of the aisle in Congress and gun advocacy groups. 

On Tuesday, President Trump criticized Pretti, saying, “You can’t have guns. You can’t walk in with guns.”

However, under Minnesota state law, registered gun owners like Pretti are permitted to carry firearms at protests. 

GOP Sen. Ted Budd (N.C.) urged federal officials to remember that “words matter.”

“Americans have a constitutional right to bear arms, and the mere possession of a firearm does not represent a threat justifying lethal force,” the senator posted on the social platform X on Tuesday. 

FBI Director Kash Patel also shifted some blame to Pretti, telling Fox News on Sunday, “You cannot bring a firearm loaded with multiple magazines to any sort of protest that you want.”

The Minnesota Gun Owners Caucus responded to Patel’s claim Sunday in a post on X.

“This is completely incorrect on Minnesota law,” the group wrote. “There is no prohibition on a permit holder carrying a firearm, loaded, with multiple magazines at a protest or rally in Minnesota.”

data.title: 'Night owls' face higher risk of heart attacks and strokes: Study
data.description:
(NewsNation) — Adults who tend to stay up late are in for a rude awakening, as a new study has linked “night owl” habits to an increased risk for heart problems.

The study, published in the Journal of the American Heart Association on Wednesday, found that middle-aged and older adults who were more active in the evenings had poorer cardiovascular health, compared to peers who were more active during the day.

Researchers asked more than 320,000 adults between 39 and 74 in the United Kingdom to categorize themselves as being a “definite morning,” “definite evening,” or “intermediate” person. Using these responses, researchers looked at the health metrics of the participants, such as their diet, weight, smoking habits, physical activity habits, blood pressure, cholesterol and other factors to calculate participants’ Life’s Essential 8 (LE8) score.

The authors found that “night owls had a 79% higher prevalence of having an overall poor cardiovascular health score.” The study also noted that “night owls had a 16% higher risk of having a heart attack or stroke over a median of about 14 years follow-up, compared to people within the intermediate category.”

But it’s not all bad news for those who go to bed late, as researchers noted that simple shifts in behaviors can improve scores.

“These findings show that the higher heart disease risks among evening types are partly due to modifiable behaviors such as smoking and sleep,” said Dr. Kristen Knutson, who is a volunteer chair of the 2025 American Heart Association. “Therefore, evening types have options to improve their cardiovascular health.”

“Evening types aren’t inherently less healthy, but they face challenges that make it particularly important for them to maintain a healthy lifestyle,” Knutson added.

data.title: Virginia Democrats appeal ruling on redistricting amendment
data.description:
Virginia Democrats on Wednesday appealed a judge’s ruling that blocks a redistricting amendment from going before voters this spring as they look to pick up House seats ahead of the midterms. 

“Today, we followed through on our commitment to appeal the Tazewell ruling,” top Virginia Democratic legislative leaders said in a joint statement. “This decision was legally flawed, unprecedented, and the product of court-shopping designed to block Virginians from voting on their own Constitution.”

“We remain confident we will prevail, and we will continue moving forward to put this question directly to the voters — where it belongs,” they added. 

A local county circuit court judge ruled on Tuesday that Virginia Democrats would not be allowed to move forward with the redistricting amendment planned to go before voters in April. If approved, the amendment would give lawmakers permission to temporarily redraw the Old Dominion’s congressional lines in time for the midterms. 

The judge argued Democrats had improperly convened for their special session and that the timing of the state legislature’s first passage of the constitutional amendment did not fall prior to the 2025 elections. 

Democrats are required to pass their constitutional amendment twice — with an election falling in between — in order for it to be teed up before voters. 

Virginia Republicans had celebrated the ruling on Tuesday while Democrats had signaled they were planning to appeal the decision.

data.title: Employee engagement slips from 2020 high: Gallup
data.description:
A new Gallup survey found that the share of respondents who reported feeling actively engaged at work last year was down 5 percent from its high in 2020. 

The poll, released Wednesday, found that 31 percent of employees said they felt actively engaged at work in 2025, the same percentage that said as much in 2024. In 2020, 36 percent of respondents said they were engaged at work, the highest mark among Gallup surveys on the topic dating back to 2000.

Gallup noted that each percentage-point change in active engagement represents roughly 1.6 million full- or part-time employees. Therefore, the 5 percent decline in employee engagement since 2020 represents roughly 8 million fewer workers feeling engaged at work.

Meanwhile, 17 percent of respondents reported feeling actively disengaged at work in 2025, a percentage unchanged from the year prior. The highest share of respondents, 20 percent, that reported feeling disengaged occurred in 2007 and 2008, amid the global financial crisis.

In 2020, 40 percent of Gen Z and younger millennials reported being engaged at work, while 39 percent of older millennials reported being engaged. But last year, 32 and 30 percent of those two groups reported feeling engaged, respectively.

Gen X saw a drop of 6 percent in the category, from 35 to 29 percent, while 34 percent of Baby Boomers reported feeling engaged last year — the same percentage that said so in 2020. 

During the COVID-19 pandemic, which began in the U.S. in 2020, the unemployment rate increased sharply to 14.8 percent in April of that year before decreasing to 6.7 percent in December according to the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS).

Last month, unemployment hit 4.4 percent, up 0.3 percent over the prior 12 months. 

The BLS also reported in 2024 that remote workers increased by more than twofold in 10 industries from 2019 to 2022. However, the bureau noted that productivity was slightly positively correlated with increasing remote workers in 61 industries during that time period. 

The Gallup survey was conducted via quarterly online surveys with 78,945 U.S. adults. It has a margin of error of 0.5 percent.

data.title: DHS review does not mention Pretti brandishing gun: Reports
data.description:
An internal review of the Alex Pretti shooting, reviewed by multiple news outlets, does not mention that he brandished his weapon before being shot, as at least one Trump official initially claimed.

The preliminary review by U.S. Customs and Border Protection (CBP) said two federal agents fired shots at Pretti after he was resisting arrest, Reuters reported.

“During the struggle, a [Border Patrol Agent] yelled, ‘He’s got a gun!’ multiple times,” the report says, according to MS Now, who reviewed the write-up sent to lawmakers on Tuesday afternoon. 

“Approximately five seconds later, a BPA discharged his CBP-issued Glock 19 and a CBPO also discharged his CBP-issued Glock 47 at Pretti. After the shooting, a BPA [Border Patrol agent] advised he had possession of Pretti’s firearm. The BPA subsequently cleared and secured Pretti’s firearm in his vehicle,” it adds.

The report does not specify exactly when Pretti’s firearm was retrieved, per MS NOW.

Immediately after the Saturday shooting, Noem said: “[Pretti] had a weapon on him, and multiple — dozens — of rounds of ammunition; wishing to inflict harm on these officers, coming, brandishing like that.”

A CBP spokesperson said the report is just an “initial outline” of the event, which drew quick outrage nationwide.

“These notifications reflect standard Customs and Border Protection protocol and are issued in accordance with existing procedures. They provide an initial outline of an event that took place and do not convey any definitive conclusion or investigative findings,” a CBP spokesperson told The Hill. 

“They are factual reports — not analytical judgments — and are provided to inform Congress and to promote transparency,” the spokesperson added.

The White House referred requests for comments to the Department of Homeland Security. 

In the wake of two fatal shootings in Minneapolis this year, lawmakers from both parties have called for Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem to resign or be fired and for a pause to immigration enforcement operations in Minnesota.

However, Trump told reporters on Tuesday that he would not fire Noem and questioned why Pretti had a weapon on him during the weekend protest.

“You can’t have guns. You can’t walk in with guns,” Trump told reporters before departing the White House for Iowa.

The president went on to call the incident “very unfortunate.”

data.title: Maine governor on ICE surge: 'We will not be intimidated'
data.description:
Maine Gov. Janet Mills (D) warned the Trump administration on Tuesday that the state “will not be intimidated” by the administration’s surge in federal immigration enforcement.

“Today, I say to the people of Maine: We will not be intimidated. We will not be silenced,” Mills said during her final State of the State address. “And to anyone outside these halls, including any federal officials, I say: If you seek to harm Maine people, you will have to go through me first.”

The Democratic governor added, “Those who may be unconcerned by the federal government’s abuses of today, I only ask you to consider this — what happens when the federal government finds you to be the problem tomorrow?” 

“This is not partisan. This is not about whether you are a Democrat, a Republican, or an independent. This is not about whether you live in a blue state, a red state, or a purple state —  a city, a suburb, or a small rural town,” she continued. “It is about Margaret Chase Smith’s principles of Americanism — it is about the very essence of America and the fundamental tenet that here power comes from the people, here the rule of law reigns, here everyone is treated equally under the law, and here we have no kings.”

The address comes days after the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) announced the start of “Operation Catch of the Day” to crack down on migrants without legal status in Maine. She requested a meeting with President Trump about the surge, calling on the administration to withdraw Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officers from the Pine Tree State.

“As Governor, I am requesting that the President of the United States meet with me so that I can demand in person that his Administration withdraw these untrained and reckless ICE agents in Maine and across the country who are stoking fear in communities, arresting legally present people, including law enforcement officials, and who pose a grave threat to public safety,” she said in a statement last week.

Mills made the request Saturday after a second fatal shooting in Minneapolis at the hands of immigration officers. Alex Pretti, an ICU nurse protesting Trump’s immigration agenda, was shot and killed by Border Patrol agents just weeks after Renee Good’s death.

The incidents have sparked mass protests throughout Minnesota and beyond. Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem has also faced scrutiny from both sides of the aisle over her handling of the agency and its use of force during immigration operations.

Democrats have threatened to launch impeachment proceedings against Noem if she opts not to resign or if the president does not fire her. Trump so far has thrown his support behind the DHS chief.

The Maine governor accused the president and Noem of weaponizing federal officers “against the people they are meant to serve.”

In an interview with Maine Public Radio on Tuesday, Sen. Susan Collins (R-Maine), who is being challenged by Mills for her seat, called on DHS to pause its immigration operations in both Maine and Minnesota “so that they could be reviewed and far more targeted in their scope.”

data.title: Fed keeps rates steady amid Trump backlash, criminal probe
data.description:
The Federal Reserve declined to cut interest rates Wednesday, holding steady amid President Trump’s battle to exert more influence over the independent central bank.

The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) voted 10 to 2 to keep its baseline interest rate range unchanged at 3.5 to 3.75 percent, with Fed board members Christopher Waller and Stephen Miran voting for a 0.25 percentage point cut.

Economists widely expected the panel of Fed rate-setters to hold fast this month and end a string of three consecutive cuts that began in September.

With inflation treading water above the Fed’s target, job growth stabilizing and gross domestic product expansion above expectations, the central bank is under less economic pressure to cut rates than it was toward the end of 2025.

But the political pressure on the Fed has reached new heights amid a Justice Department criminal probe into the bank and its chairman, Jerome Powell. The Trump administration is also pushing to fire Fed board member Lisa Cook in a Supreme Court case with serious implications for the bank’s independence.

The Fed and Powell are facing these obstacles with just four months left in his term as chair, and as Trump is to announce his potential successor.

Developing

data.title: FBI searches Fulton County elections office in Georgia
data.description:
FBI agents on Wednesday executed a search warrant at the Fulton County elections office south of Atlanta.

Agents executed “a court authorized law enforcement action” at the county’s main office in Union City, according to an FBI statement obtained by NewsNation, The Hill’s cable partner.

“Our investigation into this matter is ongoing so there are no details that we can provide at the moment,” the statement reads.

The Hill and NewsNation have reached out to the Fulton County election officials for comment.

Fulton County was the flashpoint between local prosecutors and President Trump following the 2020 election. Fulton County District Attorney Fani Willis (D) indicted Trump on racketeering charges in 2023, alleging he and 18 others conspired to subvert former President Biden’s 2020 election win in Georgia and keep Trump in office.

The case unraveled after Willis was disqualified from prosecuting the case following the revelation that she had an intimate relationship with a man she chose to lead the case. The case was dropped in 2025.

In December, the Department of Justice (DOJ) issued a complaint against Fulton County over information related to the 2020 election. The DOJ requested “all used and void ballots, stubs of all ballots, signature envelopes, and corresponding envelope digital files from the 2020 General Election in Fulton County” to investigate “compliance with federal law.”

data.title: ADL finds Grok is the worst AI chatbot at countering antisemitism
data.description:
Grok, the large language model of Elon Musk’s social platform X, came in last place in a new ranking of AI chatbots’ ability to counter antisemitic and extremist content. 

The Anti-Defamation League (ADL) said in a report released Wednesday that it ran six popular chatbots through several rounds of questioning related to anti-Jewish, anti-Zionist and extremist biases.

The tests included written statements and images, and the study rated the AI systems out of 100, with higher scores reflecting a better ability to counteract these biases. 

Grok scored a dismal overall rating of 21 on these tests. The chatbot scored 25 in its ability to detect and counter anti-Jewish bias, 18 for anti-Zionist bias and 20 for extremist biases. 

By contrast, Anthropic’s Claude had an overall score of 80, and OpenAI’s ChatGPT came in second at 57.

“This score places [Grok] in the low performance tier (<35), indicating substantial limitations in bias detection,” the ADL wrote in their report. “With a 59-point gap from the top-performing model, large improvements would likely be needed to reach competitive performance levels in bias detection and response on the issues tested.”

The Hill has reached out to X for comment.

This rating comes after Musk released a revised version of the chatbot last year following multiple reports of antisemitic responses.

The ADL expressed significant concerns about Grok’s ability to detect and counter antisemitic biases, noting the model’s “almost complete failure in image analysis” testing. 

“With an overall score in the low tier, Grok requires fundamental improvements across multiple dimensions before it can be considered useful for bias detection applications,” the report stated. 

Musk’s chatbot has also recently come under fire for allegedly generating millions of nonconsensual, sexual deepfake content, including thousands of sexually explicit images of children.

The European Commission opened an investigation into the social platform X, where Grok is accessed, on Monday. 

Following the launch of the investigation, Musk responded to an X post that questioned why ChatGPT and Google’s Gemini were not also included in the probe, saying, “Good question.”

data.title: World of Travel: Cape Town, Mpumalanga, and Lesedi Village, South Africa
data.description:
South Africa reveals itself in layers that are political, cultural, spiritual and deeply personal.

Long before the landscapes and safaris come into view, the country’s global imprint is already evident.

In the early 1990s, Cape Town quietly shaped the worldview of a young Zohran Mamdani, who spent formative years there while his father, renowned anthropologist Mahmood Mamdani, served as chair of African studies at the University of Cape Town.

Mamdani’s childhood, rooted in Islamic education at a local mosque and schooling at St. George’s Grammar, would later inform a political consciousness that now turned him into well-known political figure.

His story mirrors South Africa itself: a place that shapes how people see the world, often in ways they don’t fully realize until much later.

Curated Access to a Complex Country

That layered understanding was central to the journey organized by Travelead, the tour-planning company that curated the entire itinerary from start to finish.

With every detail thoughtfully arranged, the experience allowed the team to move seamlessly through South Africa’s contrasts, urban and wild, ancient and modern, without distraction.

Travelead specializes in immersive, culturally grounded travel, designed to dismantle misconceptions and replace them with lived experience.

World of Travel began in Mpumalanga, home to some of South Africa’s most iconic safari landscapes.

The region offers access to the famed Big Five: lion, leopard, elephant, rhino, and Cape buffalo, but the experience extends far beyond those symbols. Hundreds of species of birds, mammals, and reptiles thrive across the savannas, creating one of the most biodiverse ecosystems on Earth.

Even the youngest traveler, toddler Kynsley, joined the safari, learning early the quiet reverence required to witness wildlife in its natural habitat.

Ramie Veerappan, founder of Travelead, explained why bringing people to South Africa is deeply personal: “For me, I get very angry and upset because people look at Africa as the dark continent, like animals are roaming on the streets and I’m always trying to tell people that is not true. Come to South Africa and you’ll see some of the most beautiful cities in the world.”

Culture at the Cradle of Humanity

To understand South Africa’s cultural depth, the journey continued to Lesedi Cultural Village, located within the Cradle of Humankind, a UNESCO World Heritage Site home to the earliest traces of humanity.

Named after King Moshoeshoe’s words, “Our culture is the light of our nation,” Lesedi serves as a living custodian of African history.

Through recreated homesteads, storytelling, music, dance, and traditional cuisine, visitors gain insight into the Zulu, Xhosa, Pedi, Basotho, and Ndebele cultures, experiencing South Africa not as a monolith, but as a medley of nations within one country.

As night fell in the bush, the experience deepened with a Bush Braai, a quintessential South African tradition blending a sunset game drive with an open-air barbecue beneath the stars.

Gathered around a bonfire, with the sounds of the wilderness echoing nearby, the evening offered a sensory reminder that culture here is inseparable from land.

Cape Town: Where History, Art, and Nature Converge

In Cape Town, the story shifted again.

Scholar Dr. Trishula Patel, assistant professor of African and African Diaspora history at the University of Denver, challenged the narrow lens through which South Africa is often viewed.

“There’s always this idea that South Africa is only about safari and you go there to see wild animals and yes, they have some of the best national parks, but there’s so much more to South Africa than that,” she said. “A common misconception is that South Africa is underdeveloped, that it’s not up to Western standards and anyone who travels to South Africa can immediately see that’s not true.

“The region is one of the most educated in the world. The culture it produces is some of the most diverse and beautiful and South Africa has beautiful cities and not just naturally beautiful landscapes.”

Patel also contextualized Cape Town’s global influence: “Cape Town is one of the most global cities out there. That is absolutely because of its location which historically was important for trade before the Suez Canal existed. You had to go around it to get from one side of the world to the other.”

“You have places like Cape Town that are mountainous but also almost Mediterranean in climate, which led to it becoming the hub for the South African wine industry,” Patel added.

Wine, Land, and Reclaimed Legacy

That history of land and labor came into sharp focus in the Franschhoek Valley at Klein Goederust, owned by Paul Siguqa. His journey into winemaking is inseparable from South Africa’s past.

Raised on the nearby Backsberg farm, Siguqa grew up watching his mother, Nomaroma Siguqa, work as a vineyard laborer for more than 30 years during apartheid.

“For many years Black people in this area have only been looked at as laborers. My mother says it stops with her, we need to change this narrative. She was passionate about that,” Siguqa said.

He revealed that he only told his mother he had purchased the winery once the title deed was officially in hand.

“This farm was on the market for 8 years, but even though we had a cash offer it took a really long time until our offer was accepted, so I didn’t want to raise her hopes. It was unthinkable that a Black person would own land in this way,” he said.

In 2019, Siguqa officially acquired the 25-acre farm, making Klein Goederust the first 100 percent Black-owned wine farm in the Franschhoek Valley.

To honor both heritage and history, he retained the farm’s original name, established in 1905, while adding his IsiXhosa identity through the Southern Ground Hornbill, his family clan totem, now embedded in the logo. His ownership stands as both reclamation and invitation, opening doors for future generations of winemakers of color.

Art as Identity at The Silo Hotel

In Cape Town’s V&A Waterfront, The Silo Hotel stands as one of the city’s most imaginative creative spaces. Housed within a converted 100-year-old grain silo, the architectural landmark rises above the Zeitz Museum of Contemporary Art Africa (MOCAA), home to the largest collection of contemporary African art on the continent.

“This is a 100-year-old iconic building,” said General Manager Luis Pinheiro, underscoring the transformation of an industrial past into a modern sanctuary. The hotel’s pillowed-glass windows frame sweeping views of Table Mountain and Table Bay, while its interiors celebrate art, design, and African creativity.

Art Concierge Michael Jacobs explained how the hotel’s exhibitions reflect broader national conversations.

“The conversation around identity — South Africa is called the Rainbow Nation because there are so many different cultures that come together to be within community together. The conversation about land also comes out in art, we always see landscapes throughout art. And then there’s a conversation around labor. If you look at where we are now, in a converted industrial building that played quite a significant role in the industrial revolution of the country,” Jacobs said.

Each year, The Silo Hotel collaborates with a local gallery to highlight African artists through curated exhibitions in The Vault, turning hospitality into an ongoing cultural exchange.

Sound, Spirit, and the Mountain

No visit to Cape Town is complete without ascending Table Mountain, which Nelson Mandela once called a “gift to earth.” The mountain’s spiritual pull is undeniable, making it a natural home for spaces devoted to restoration and mindfulness.

Nestled on its slopes, Future Found Sanctuary offers immersive wellness experiences designed for deep relaxation and renewal.

Located within Table Mountain National Park, a UNESCO World Heritage Site, the sanctuary balances raw natural beauty with serene intention. Here, a sound bath, using bowls and gongs, created a moment of stillness, grounding the journey in reflection.

Nights of Sound and Celebration

As daylight faded, Cape Town’s nightlife told another story, one of sound, rhythm, and modern identity.

To experience the pulse of South African music, nights were spent at Souk and Drama in Cape Town, where Amapiano, Gqom, Afro-pop, and South African hip-hop filled the air.

These unforgettable evenings, shared with family and friends, revealed how music continues to shape contemporary culture, blending joy, resistance and global influence into every beat.

Living the Destination

Throughout the journey, accommodations became part of the storytelling.

Bali & Eva Luxury Collection, founded by Busi Tshili, reimagines short-term rentals as cultural experiences. Her properties, rich with art, nature, and a sense of place, aim to capture the layered history of South Africa while offering comfort and elegance.

“I don’t believe you can come to South Africa once on one holiday and experience everything. Every time you come there is something new to discover,” Tshili reflected.

From safari plains to wine valleys, from sound baths to dance floors, South Africa revealed itself not as a single story, but as many, intertwined, unresolved, and alive. Visiting South Africa allowed us to see the country as it truly is: complex, creative, and constantly redefining itself.

EDITOR’S NOTE: Lindsey Granger and Kolyn Boyd are the co-creators of “World of Travel,” a series that explores the past, present and future of extraordinary places around the globe. You can watch South Africa: Uncorked Soul, Sea & Safari and future episodes each Wednesday at 4 p.m. EDT on The Hill’s YouTube page.

Lindsey Granger is the co-host of “Rising.” Kolyn Boyd is a film director based in Washington, D.C.

Catch complimentary podcasts every Thursday at 4 p.m. on The Hill’s YouTube channel.

The South Africa episode of “World of Travel” was sponsored by Travelead, The Silo Hotel, Future Found Sanctuary and the Bali + Eva Luxury Collection
